content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string SSS consisting only of English lowercase letters (`a` - `z`). However, Hitesh does not like it and wants it to be reversed.

Hitesh wonders what is the minimum number of operations required to reverse the string SSS using the following operation:


Select any iii such that 1≤i≤∣S∣1 \le i \le \lvert S \rvert1≤i≤∣S∣ and remove SiS_iSi​ from its original position and append it to the end of the string (i.e. shift any character to the end of the string).
For e.g. if S=S =S= `abcde` and we apply operation on i=2i = 2i=2, then SSS becomes `acdeb`.

Help Hitesh find the minimum number of operations required to reverse SSS.

It is guaranteed that it is possible to reverse the string in a finite number of operations.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer  TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of  TTT  test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line containing the string S.


Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum number of operations required to reverse the string SSS.
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting only of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of operations required to transform S into its reverse.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "abdeba", the reverse is "abedba". The longest prefix of the reverse that appears as a subsequence in the original is "abe" (length 3). Hence 6‑3 = 3 moves. For "codechef", only the first character of the reverse ("f") can be kept, so 8‑1 = 7 moves.

solution.approach: Let R be the reverse of S. We need the longest prefix of R that already appears as a subsequence in S. Those characters can stay in their original positions; all other characters are moved to the end (one operation per character). Because moved characters can be taken in any order, the remaining suffix of R can always be arranged. Therefore answer = |S| - L, where L is the length of the longest prefix of R that is a subsequence of S. L can be found greedily: scan S from left to right with a pointer j on R; whenever S[i] == R[j] increment j. After the scan, j is the maximal L. The algorithm runs in O(|S|) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N = |S|. Overall O(total length of all strings).
